name: mmisp-lib
documentation:
# Modern MISP - API

[![Ruff](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/astral-sh/ruff/main/assets/badge/v2.json)](https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff) [![Conventional Commits](https://img.shields.io/badge/Conventional_Commits-1.0.0-orange.svg)](https://conventionalcommits.org)

## Requirements

- [Docker](https://www.docker.com) `latest-stable`

## Getting Started

Clone the project and install Python version `3.11.0`. It is recommended to install Python using [pyenv](https://github.com/pyenv/pyenv#installation). Then install all dependencies by typing `make setup` into your terminal and start your local database container using `make up`.

Create a file called `.env` and copy the contents of `.env.example` into it. Finally, start the development server using `make dev`.

You should now be able to access the api on `localhost:4000`.

To use the commandline tool run `python .\src\mmisp\commandline_tool\main.py --h` from the base directory to view all commands. For detailed information of all commands: `python .\src\mmisp\commandline_tool\main.py --help`.
For setting up the DB use the commandline tool option for setup and then create your user account. In case you have not created a new organisation yet, please add your account to the setup basic organisation: ghost_org.

Run tests using `make test` (local database container required running) or `make test/lite`.

## Setting up your IDE

Be sure to use the newly created virtual env as your interpreter (`./venv/bin/python`). Also install the [Ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/integrations/) extension for your IDE and set `Ruff` as your default code formatter. It is recommended to activate formatting your code on every save.

## Best Practices

### General Guidelines

The following are some guidelines for writing code, in no particular order:

- Try to write clean code
- Use the "early return" pattern, do you really need that `else` block?
- Add correct types wherever possible, reduce `Any` occurrences as much as possible
- Reduce database calls
- Be consistent within your code, and within the rest of the codebase
- Use whitespace generously, to group and separate lines of code
- Be explicit, magic is great until it is not

### Endpoint Ordering

Try to order endpoints using CRUD so that the following order is achieved:

- Create a {resource}
- Read / Get a {resource}
- Update a {resource}
- Delete a {resource}
- Get all {resource}s
- More niche endpoints
- Deprecated endpoints

functions.name: mmisp.lib.logger.log
documentation:
Log decorator for synchronous functions.

See alog for more information.

functions.name: mmisp.lib.logger.alog
documentation:
Log decorator for fastapi routes and asynchronous functions.

Since we are using return await func, this only works for async functions
inspect.isawaitable returns False on the decorated functions, so we cannot
make it usable for both worlds.

methods.name: mmisp.api_schemas.events.AddEditGetEventDetails.zero_sharing_group_id_to_none
documentation: @validator("uuid", "extends_uuid", pre=True)
@classmethod
def uuid_empty_str(cls: Type["AddEditGetEventDetails"], value: Any) -> Any:  # noqa: ANN102
Method to convert an empty string or None to a UUID filled with zeros for the UUID fields.
:param value: the value to check and possibly convert
:type value: Any
:return: returns a UUID object containing zeros if the input is an empty string,zero or None
otherwise the input value
:rtype: Any
if value == "" or value is None or value == "0":
return value

classes.name: mmisp.api_schemas.responses.check_graph_response.IsAcyclic
documentation:
Represents the whether graph is acyclic and details of the first detected cycle.

- **is_acyclic**: False if the graph contains at least one cycle.
- **cycles**: A list of entries, each containing two node IDs and a "Cycle" string.
Conbined they result in the cycle.

Example:
```json
"is_acyclic": {
    "is_acyclic": false,
    "cycles": [
        [
            4,
            3,
            "Cycle"
        ],
        [
            3,
            4,
            "Cycle"
        ]
    ]
}
```

classes.name: mmisp.api_schemas.responses.check_graph_response.MultipleOutputConnection
documentation:
Represents the status and details of nodes with illegal multiple output connections in a graph.

- **has_multiple_output_connection**: True if at least one node has multiple output
  connections that are not allowed.
  For example, the 'Concurrent Task' node can have multiple output connections while the value here is `False`.
- **edges**: A dictionary where the key is the ID of a node with multiple illegal connections,
  and the value is a list of node IDs to which these illegal connections are made.

Example:
```json
"multiple_output_connection": {
    "has_multiple_output_connection": true,
    "edges": {
        "1": [
            5,
            3
        ]
    }
}
```

class_variables.name: mmisp.api_schemas.responses.check_graph_response.PathWarningsInfo.source_id
documentation: is a string in legacy misp

class_variables.name: mmisp.api_schemas.responses.check_graph_response.PathWarningsInfo.next_node_id
documentation: is a string in legacy misp

classes.name: mmisp.api_schemas.responses.check_graph_response.PathWarnings
documentation:
Represents warnings for paths in a graph.

- **has_path_warnings**: True if the graph contains at least one warning.
- **edges**: A list containing all connections which are flagged as warnings.

Example:
```json
"path_warnings": {
    "has_path_warnings": true,
    "edges": [
        [
            5,
            2,
            "This path leads to a blocking node from a non-blocking context",
            true,
            "stop-execution",
            2
        ]
    ]
}
```

functions.name: mmisp.commandline_tool.main.setup_db
documentation: setup
This is a simple command line tool that uses the fire library to create a command line tool for creating users
and organisations and changing their details.

functions.name: mmisp.commandline_tool.main.create_user
documentation: create-user <email> <password> <organisation> [-r <role>]

functions.name: mmisp.commandline_tool.main.create_organisation
documentation:
create-organisation <name> [-admin_email <admin_email>] [- description <description>] [-type <type>]
[-nationality <nationality>] [<sector>] [<contacts_email>] [-local <local>]
[- restricted_domain <restricted_domain>] [-landigpage <landingpage>]

functions.name: mmisp.commandline_tool.main.change_password
documentation: change-password <email> <password>

functions.name: mmisp.commandline_tool.main.change_email
documentation: change-email <email> <new_email>

functions.name: mmisp.commandline_tool.main.change_role
documentation: change-role <email> <role>

functions.name: mmisp.commandline_tool.main.edit_organisation

edit-organisation <organisation> [-new_name <new_name>] [-admin_email <admin_email>] [-description <description>]
[-type <type>] [-nationality <nationality>] [-sector <sector>] [-contacts_email <contacts_email>] [-local <local>]
[-restricted_domain <restricted_domain>] [-landingpage <landingpage>]

class_variables.name: mmisp.db.models.workflow_blueprint.WorkflowBlueprint.default
documentation: TODO: technically tinyint(1)

A python class representation of the database model for blueprints of workflows in MISP.

The most attributes of this model are similar to the attributes of workflows,
except the attributes "enabled", "counter", "trigger_id" and "debug_enabled", because these
attributes are not useful or sensible for blueprints.

Also, the attribute "default" is added, which is a boolean clarifying whether the blueprint
is a default MISP blueprint.

classes.name: mmisp.db.models.log.Log
documentation:
A python class representation of the database model for logs in MISP.

Further explanation for some of the central attributes of the database model:
- Action: Describes the action that was logged, e.g. a login or workflow execution
- Change: A string-representation of the changes made to the logged object or of
          central information about the logged object.

classes.name: mmisp.db.models.workflow.Workflow
documentation:
A python class representation of the database model for workflows in MISP.

The most central of the attributes in this model is the data attribute,
containing the information about the workflow structure and the modules contained in the workflow,
represented/stored as a JSON-String.
(The other attributes are what their name sais, e.g. counter represents the numer
of times the workflow was executed.)

methods.name: mmisp.db.mixins.DictMixin.asdict
documentation:
Return the object as dictionary.
Note that attributes starting with `_` are always omited.
Omit keys present in *omit*.

methods.name: mmisp.db.mixins.UpdateMixin.put
documentation:
Update all model fields with provided keyword arguments or set them to none.

modules.name: mmisp.db.config
documentation:
This module handels the parameters to connect to the database.

The following environment variables are supported:

- DATABASE_URL
- DEBUG
- DB_RETRY: Time to wait before trying new connection attempt. (Default 5)
- DB_MAX_RETRIES: Limit for retry attempts. (Default 100)

modules.name: mmisp.workflows.fastapi
documentation:
API schemas for workflows. No classes here, just transformers.

Intended use for incoming JSON is

```python
async def update_workflow(
    workflow: Annotated[Workflow, Depends(json_dict_to_workflow_entity)]
):
    pass
```

and for returning a JSON representation for a workflow:
```python
async def get_workflow(
    ...
) -> Annotated[Workflow, Depends(workflow_entity_to_json_dict)]:
    pass
```

modules.name: mmisp.workflows.input
documentation:
Data structure for the payload passed to the workflow and
filtering mechanism associated with it.

functions.name: mmisp.workflows.input.extract_path
documentation:
A utility method providing the Hash::extract functionality in CakePHP.

Arguments:
    path: The hash path to extract data from.
    data: The container to extract data from.
Returns:
    A list of the extracted data, if data matching the hash path was found, an empty List otherwise.

functions.name: mmisp.workflows.input.get_path
documentation:
A utility method providing the Hash::get functionality in CakePHP.

Arguments:
    path: The hash path to extract data from.
    data: The container to extract data from.
Returns:
    The extracted data, if data matching the hash path was found, a None pointer otherwise.

functions.name: mmisp.workflows.input.evaluate_condition
documentation:
A utility method for performing comparisons between the specified data.

Arguments:
    left: The first operand.
    operator: The operator to be used in the comparison.
    right: The second operand.
    impl: Which legacy MISP implementation of evaluate condition to be used: the if/else or the filter.
Returns:
    Whether the comparison holds or not.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.input.Operator

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.input.Operator.from_str
documentation:
Returns a member of this enum given the string
representation of the operator.

Arguments:
    input: string representation of the operator.
Enum representing possible filter operations.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.input.EvaluateImplementation
documentation: Enum representing all EvaluateCondition implementations in Legacy MISP.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.input.FilterError
documentation: Abstract class representing possible invalid Filter inputs

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.input.InvalidSelectionError
documentation:
If the selection passed to the filter is invalid this error is returned.
Examples for invalid selections are datatypes other from String, hashpahts,
that dont lead to a list or empty strings

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.input.InvalidPathError
documentation:
If the path passed to the filter is invalid this error is returned.
e.g.: empty String

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.input.InvalidOperationError
documentation:
If the operation passed to the filter is invalid this error is returned.
e.g.: Operation.to_str fails to return a valid operation.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.input.InvalidValueError
documentation:
If the value passed to the filter is invalid this error is returned.
e.g.: input not of type str or List[str]

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.input.Filter

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.input.Filter.match_value
documentation:
Check if a value matches a filter.

Arguments:
    value: The value to check.
    filter: The filter to match against.

Returns:
    True if the value matches the filter, False otherwise.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.input.Filter.selector
mmisp.workflows.input.Filter.path
documentation:
Attribute path in the list where each item will be
checked against `value` using operation `operator`.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.input.Filter.operator
documentation:
[`Operator`][mmisp.workflows.input.Operator] to compare
the item below `path` against `value`.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.input.Filter.value
documentation:
Value to compare against. Can be a list for operations
`in`/`not_in` or a string value for `equals`/etc..

The data passed to a workflow can be filtered on-demand.
That means, all entries from the dict may be removed that
don't match a condition.

The condition that needs to match is represented by
this object.

There are two ways these filters can be applied:

* via the
  [`ModuleGenericFilterData`][mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleGenericFilterData]
  in place. Can be undone by adding
  [`ModuleGenericFilterReset`][mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleGenericFilterReset]
  later on.

* via a module with `on_demand_filtering_enabled` set to
  `True`. The filtering must be called by the module itself
  in that case.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.input.WorkflowInput

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.input.WorkflowInput.data
documentation:
Returns either all of the data given to the workflow input
OR a list with filter results if a filter was added
using [`WorkflowInput.add_filter`][mmisp.workflows.input.WorkflowInput.add_filter].

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.input.WorkflowInput.add_filter
documentation:
Adds another [`Filter`][mmisp.workflows.input.Filter]
to the workflow input.

Arguments:
    filter: Filter to be added.

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.input.WorkflowInput.reset_filters
documentation:
Removes all filters from the workflow input.
[`WorkflowInput.data`][mmisp.workflows.input.WorkflowInput.data]
will contain all of the data it has instead of a
filtered portion now.

When a workflow gets executed, it gets input data. For
instance, the "after event save"-workflow gets a dictionary
containing the event that was saved.

Additionally, filters can be added by modules. That way,
subsequent modules will only see a filtered subset
of the workflow data. This operation can be undone.

Filters are expressed using [`Filter`][mmisp.workflows.input.Filter]
class.

modules.name: mmisp.workflows.graph
documentation:
This package contains the data-structure representing the workflow graph
and the modules in it.

The sub-packages are specialisations, in this package the basic graph representation
is implemented.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Apperance

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Apperance.pos
documentation: Tuple with x/y coordinates in the canvas where the current node is displayed.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Apperance.typenode
documentation: Unclear what exactly that is. Hardcoded to `False` for now since it's like this everywhere.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Apperance.cssClass
documentation: CSS classes that the current node has in the visual editor.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Apperance.nodeUid
documentation:
Unique ID in the DOM set by the visual editor. Sometimes it's apparently `None` for
unknown reasons.
Value object with attributes that are only used in the visual editor.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.GraphError
documentation:
Abstract base-class for validation errors in the graph structure. The idea is to
display error kinds via

```python
match error:
    case CyclicGraphError(path):
        pass
    case MultipleEdgesPerOutput(affected):
        pass
    # ...
```

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.CyclicGraphError

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.CyclicGraphError.cycle_path
documentation: List of edges (from, output_port, to, input_port) that create a cycle.
If a cycle was found in the graph it is invalid. This class represents that error.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.MultipleEdgesPerOutput

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.MultipleEdgesPerOutput.affected
documentation:
All edges affected by the problem: first component of the tuple
is the node with too many connections per output, the second
component is the ID of the affected output.

A [`Node`][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node] has multiple inputs and outputs. Unless
explicitly allowed, each output may only have a single connection to another node.
An example for that is [`ModuleConcurrentTask`][mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleConcurrentTask].

This class represents the error if that's not allowed.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.MissingTrigger
documentation:
Each workflow that isn't a blueprint must have a trigger as starting node.
A violation of that is described with this class.

!!! note
    Incidentally, in legacy MISP this isn't part of `checkGraph`, but
    only used in the validation rules of the `Workflow` model.

    In MMISP, both validations are unified.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.UnsupportedWorkflow

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.UnsupportedWorkflow.module_graph_id
documentation: ID of the unsupported module

Not all modules are implemented in modern MISP. All of them exist though
to allow reading any workflow JSON here.

If a workflow relies on unsupported modules, this error class is used.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.ConfigurationError

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.ConfigurationError.node
documentation: Reference to the misconfigured node.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.ConfigurationError.context
documentation: Error message.

Generic "configuration error" class. Useful to e.g. reject wrong parameters
from the visual editor.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.InconsistentEdgeBetweenAdjacencyLists

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.InconsistentEdgeBetweenAdjacencyLists.from_
documentation: The starting node of the inconsistent edge.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.InconsistentEdgeBetweenAdjacencyLists.to
documentation: The ending node of the inconsistent edge.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.InconsistentEdgeBetweenAdjacencyLists.input_port_id
documentation: The input port of the inconsistent edge.

In MISP the standard for storing the graph is in duplicated form, the graph is stored once as an adjacency list of
outgoing edges and in parallel a second time as an adjacency list of incoming edges.
In case there is an edge from node A to node B, that is registered as outgoing from node A to node B and not as
incoming from node A to node B or vica-versa, this is an inconsistency error that will be returned with an instance
of this class.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.GraphValidationResult

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.GraphValidationResult.add_result
documentation:
Merges another validation result into this accumulator object.

Arguments:
    other: Another validation result added in here.

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.GraphValidationResult.is_valid
documentation: If neither errors nor warnings are added, the graph is valid.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.GraphValidationResult.errors
documentation: Fatal errors in the workflow graph. The graph is unusable like this.

Accumulator for all validation results. The idea is to run graph-level checks such
as cycle-detection in [`Graph.check`][mmisp.workflows.graph.Graph.check] and then run node-level
checks in [`Node.check`][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.check]. Whenever an error is encountered, it's
added into this class.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.__eq__
documentation:
Custom comparison if two instances are equal, needed for our special hash function so that the statement
__eq__(a, b) = True => __hash__(a) == __hash__(b) is valid for every pair of nodes a nd b, which guaranties that
the Dict (HashMap) will not malfunction.

Arguments:
    other: A reference to the other object to be compared.

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.__hash__
documentation: Our custom node hash function which allows to store nodes in a Dict (HashMap).

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.check
documentation:
Checks if the module is correctly configured. At Node level 3 conditions are checked (and errors returned if not
fulfilled):
1(2). The registered input(output) ports are in the range from 1 to
['n_inputs'][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.n_inputs](['n_outputs'][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.n_outputs]) incl.
It is allowed to have less registered input(output) ports than the specified maximum amount.
3. In case ['enable_multiple_edges_per_output'][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.enable_multiple_edges_per_output] has
a value of false, each outgoing port has maximum 1 outgoing edge. (It is allowed for an outgoing port to have no
outgoing edges.)

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.inputs
documentation:
References to [`Node`][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node] objects connecting to this
node. Grouped by the ID of the input.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.outputs
documentation:
References to [`Node`][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node] objects that are connected to
this node. Grouped by the ID of the output.

The data-structure is recursive here (basically a doubly-linked list)
using references to make walking the graph as easy as possible.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.graph_id
documentation: The id with which this node was identified in the json data, that was used to initialize its parent graph.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.n_inputs
documentation:
Number of _allowed_ inputs. If `inputs` exceeds this, the
[`Node.check`][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.check] method will return an error for this.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.n_outputs
documentation:
Number of _allowed_ outputs. If `outputs` exceeds this, the
[`Node.check`][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.check] method will return an error for this.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.enable_multiple_edges_per_output
documentation:
Whether it's OK to have multiple edges going from a single output.
See [`Node`][mmisp.workflows.graph.Node] for more context. Used by e.g. the concurrent
tasks module.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Node.supported
documentation:
Indicator whether the module / trigger is supported. All items from legacy MISP
exist here to allow loading any workflow, but not all are actually supported by
the application.

A single node in a graph. Can be a trigger - something that caused the workflow run -
or a module - a behavioral component in the workflow graph.

Inputs & outputs can be thought of like a layer 4 address where the node is the
address and the input/output is the port number. So an edge doesn't just connect
two nodes together, but tuples of `(output number, input)` and
`(input number, output)`. This is needed because

* The concurrent execution requires just a single output, but with multiple edges
  to other modules from it.
* An If/Else module has two outputs: one node to execute if the condition is
  true and one if it isn't.

It might've been possible to infer outputs by the order of the connections, but this
would've lead to ambiguity because

* If >1 module is connected to this module, it's not clear whether there are multiple
  connections leaving the same output (as in the concurrency module) or if each module
  has its own meaning (as in the if/else module).
* It's valid to produce an if/else module with only else connecting to another node.
  In that case the first output in the list should actually be encoded as
  second list element since the first is empty.

Because of that, infering inputs/outputs from the order seemed too error-prone
and the legacy MISP structure was reused here.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.WorkflowNode

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.WorkflowNode.id
documentation:
Unique identifier of the module/trigger. To be declared in the
subclass implementing a concrete module/trigger.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.WorkflowNode.name
documentation:
Human-readable identifier of the module/trigger. To be declared in the
subclass implementing a concrete module/trigger.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.WorkflowNode.blocking
documentation:
If the workflow of a blocking trigger fails, the actual
operation won't be carried out. For instance, if the
"event before save"-trigger fails, the event will not
besaved.

For modules, blocking and failing modules will terminate
the workflow execution.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.WorkflowNode.version
documentation:
Current version of the module. To be declared in the
subclass implementing a concrete module.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.WorkflowNode.apperance
documentation: Value object with miscellaneous settings for the visual editor.

Dataclass with shared properties for both modules & triggers,
but not relevant for the abstract Node type.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.initialize_for_visual_editor
documentation:
Initializes the parameters for a module. Done in a method
since that may involve further DB operations.

Only needed for the visual editor, not the execution since it
defines which params are expected and which values are accepted
(e.g. all attributes stored in the database).

Arguments:
    db: SQLAlchemy session

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.is_initialized_for_visual_editor
documentation:
Checks if the module was initialized for the visual editor
which happens by calling
[`Module.initialize`][mmisp.workflows.modules.Module.initialize].
It's expected that the attribute
`params` will be set by this method.

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.exec
documentation:
Executes the module using the specific payload given by the workflow that calls
the execution of the module.
Execution strongly depends on the type of the module that is executed.

The first component of the tuple indicates whether execution was successful.
The second component of the tuple is the next node to be executed.

Since this library allows arbitrarily many inputs & outputs, we cannot infer
the next module from the success of the execution of this module (relevant for
e.g. if/else).

The default implementation assumes exactly one output and returns it on success.

Arguments:
    payload: The workflows input for the specific module execution.
    db: Database handle for write operations.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.configuration
documentation: Values for the params required by this module.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.template_params
documentation: List containing all params that are jinja2 templates.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.on_demand_filter
documentation:
Some modules allow filtering of data internally without modifying
[`WorkflowInput`][mmisp.workflows.input.WorkflowInput]. The filter
used for that is defined by this attribute.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.previous_version
documentation: Previously used version of the module.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.html_template
documentation:
HTML template provided by the visual editor to use.
To be declared in the subclass implementing a concrete module.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.on_demand_filtering_enabled
documentation: Whether internal filtering is enabled.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.blocking
documentation:
Whether or not the module is blocking, i.e. can abort the
workflow if it fails. If it's not blocking and fails,
execution continues.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.description
documentation:
Human-readable description of the node.
Must be here because Modules and triggers have a description.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Module.icon
documentation:
Frontend icon.
Must be here because Modules and triggers have a description.

A module is a workflow node that is either

* an action, i.e. performs an operation with an observable effect
* a logic module, i.e. forwards to a different module based on
  a condition
* a filter, i.e. manipulates the
  [`WorkflowInput`][mmisp.workflows.input.WorkflowInput].

A module implementation can be provided by writing a subclass that
assigns values to at least `id`, `version`, `name`.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Trigger

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Trigger.normalize_data
documentation:
Allows triggers to perform custom "normalization" operations
before handing over to the actual modules.

Workflow input can be an arbitrary JSON already, but it can also be
a SQLAlchemy model. In the latter case it's this method's responsibility
to transform it into MISP compliant data.

Arguments:
    db:     DB handle to load more entities from the database.
    input:  Workflow input to modify.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Trigger.scope
documentation: Scope the trigger operates on. E.g. `event` or `attribute`.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Trigger.description
documentation: Human-readable description of when the trigger gets executed.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Trigger.overhead
documentation:
Indicates the expensiveness/overhead of the trigger
as indicated by the [`Overhead`][mmisp.workflows.modules.Overhead] enum.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Trigger.overhead_message
documentation:
Explanatory message describing why the overhead level was chosen
for this trigger.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Trigger.icon
documentation: Frontend icon.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Trigger.disabled
documentation: Whether or not the trigger is disabled, i.e. ignored when triggered.

A trigger represents the starting point of a workflow.
It can have only one output and no inputs. Each trigger
is represented by this class, the attributes are loaded
from the DB.

Legacy MISP provides subclasses for each triggers, but
since the properties were saved into the database anyways
and no behavior was added to those classes, the idea was
dropped entirely in MMISP.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Frame

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Frame.text
documentation: Title of the frame

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Frame.uuid
documentation: Unique identifier of the frame. Used by the visual editor.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Frame.clazz
documentation: Additional CSS classes for the frame. `class` is a reserved keyword.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Frame.nodes
documentation:
List of (references) to nodes that are visually encapsulated in the frame
in the visual editor.
Value object representing a frame in the visual editor. Only for visualization.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Graph

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Graph.check
documentation:
Checks if the graph's structure is valid. Works as described in
[`GraphValidationResult`][mmisp.workflows.graph.GraphValidationResult].

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Graph.initialize_graph_modules
documentation:
This method is declared in `mmisp.workflows.modules`, but
is a method of is part of \[`Graph`\][mmisp.workflows.graph.Graph].
It injects `db` into each module. This is done to avoid
circular import situations.

!!! note

: For now, this is necessary since modules may require other
  objects from the database. E.g. the
  \[`ModuleOrganisationIf`\][mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleOrganisationIf]
  loads the names of all organisations.

  There are a few ways forward:

  - Implement a more sophisticated SQLAlchemy type that
    allows to query other entities while placing the JSON
    into the \[`Graph`\][mmisp.workflows.graph.Graph] structure.
  - Factor the {attribute}`mmisp.workflows.modules.Module.params`
    structure out and inject it into the workflow editor on its own.

  For modernizing legacy MISP's workflows and retaining backwards
  compatibility, just injecting the DB into each node at the
  beginning is the simplest solution though.

Arguments:

: db: SQLAlchemy DB session.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Graph.nodes
documentation:
A list of all nodes inside the graph. Edges are represented by nodes
having references to other nodes in their
`inputs`/`outputs`.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Graph.root
documentation: Reference to the root of the graph.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.Graph.frames
documentation: List of frame objects in the visual editor.
A representation of a workflow graph. Consists of

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.WorkflowGraph
documentation:
This graph represents a workflow. That means that it MUST have a trigger as starting
node and no trigger anywhere else.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.BlueprintGraph

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.graph.BlueprintGraph.check
documentation: Custom check method for blueprint graph.

This graph represents a blueprint. Blueprints are reusable parts of a
workflow graph and MUST NOT have a trigger.

Whether that's the case can be enforced with the
[`BlueprintGraph.check`][mmisp.workflows.graph.BlueprintGraph.check] method.

modules.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy
documentation:
All other modules intended to be agnostic of old legacy MISP formats
as much as possible and can be refactored gradually into a more reasonable
structure.

This is the compat-layer with legacy MISP: it understands the old JSON and
imports/exports that into the new data structure.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.GraphValidation

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.GraphValidation.report_as_str
documentation:
Turns validation report into a string. Used for legacy `/workflows/edit`.
The frontend expects a single error message here in a modal in the top left corner,
so all errors are kept brief on purpose.

Arguments:
    result: Validation report.
    graph:  Graph that got validated.

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.GraphValidation.report
documentation:
Reports the results of the graph validation.
Returns a CheckGraphResponse object containing all the results.
NB! The current API endpoint implementation does not allow multiple cycles to be returned, therefore only the
first found cycle will be returned. others are discarded. To be changed in the future when the API endpoint is
updated. The PathWarning system is deprecated and not used in the modern MISP, therefore in the JSON will be
always set that there are no PathWarnings.

Arguments:
    result:     GraphValidationResult object containing the results of the graph validation.
This class checks whether a graph is valid or not.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.GraphFactory

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.GraphFactory.graph2jsondict
documentation:
Converts a modern MISP graph object to a graph in the legacy MISP JSON format used in the DB.

Arguments:
    graph:  Graph object to convert to JSON.

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.GraphFactory.jsondict2graph
documentation:
Converts a graph in the legacy MIPS JSON format to a graph object.
Returns the input graph as a modern MISP graph object.

Arguments:
    input: JSON dictionary containing the graph information.

This class is responsible for creating a MMSIP graph object from the legacy MISP JSON format.
The class can also convert a graph in the legacy MISP JSON format from the DB to a modern graph
object.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.JSONGraphType

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.JSONGraphType.get_col_spec
documentation: Returns the colum specification for the custom SQLAlchemy type in LONGTEXT.

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.JSONGraphType.bind_processor
documentation:
Method for processing data before storing it in the database.

Arguments:
    dialect:    SQLAlchemy dialect being used.

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.legacy.JSONGraphType.result_processor
documentation:
Defines how to process data retrieved from the database.
Converts the JSON string stored in the database back into a graph object
using the GraphFactory's jsondict2graph method.

Arguments:
    dialect:    SQLAlchemy dialect being used.
    coltype:    Type of the column being processed.

SQLAlchemy type used to store and retrieve graph objects in the database.
This type handles the conversion between a modern MISP graph object and its JSON
representation for the compatibility with legacy MISP.

modules.name: mmisp.workflows.execution
documentation: Models related to the execution of workflows.

functions.name: mmisp.workflows.execution.walk_nodes
documentation:
Recursive graph walker implementation starting at a given node.
Used by the workflow execution itself, but can also be used to resume
workflow execution, e.g. for concurrent modules that schedule jobs
with the successor nodes.

Arguments:
    input:          Workflow payload for `current_node`.
    current_node:   Node to resume execution with.
    workflow:       Workflow entity. Used for logging.
    logger:         Application logger to write debug messages and errors
        from the execution.
    db:             Database session.
    jinja2_engine:  Instantiated templating engine to substitute placeholders
        in module configuration with values from the payload.

functions.name: mmisp.workflows.execution.execute_workflow
documentation:
Provides the functionality for executing a workflow, which consists of traversing
the given workflow graph and its modules and executing these modules with their specific
configurations.

!!! note
    Legacy MISP allows non-blocking paths, i.e. multiple "roots" & no
    termination of the workflow execution if one of the paths fails.

    This "feature" is left out entirely here: this would not only break
    the assumption of having a single root, but also complicate the execution
    code further.

    This is only implemented for concurrent tasks, however

    * A job will be exposed in worker that allows to resume execution of
      a workflow at a given node. This is triggered for each of the concurrent
      nodes.

    * No intertwined state since all the state of an execution is carried around via
      the payload.

Arguments:
    workflow: The Graph representation of the workflow to be executed.
    input:    Initial payload for the workflow.
    db:       SQLAlchemy session.
    logger:   Application logger to notify about errors or (if debug is enabled) execution steps.

modules.name: mmisp.workflows.modules
documentation:
This module contains subclasses for different node-types,
i.e. triggers and modules and implementations for modules
that were bundled with legacy MISP.

functions.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.workflow_node
documentation:
Annotation that registers the annotated class in the
[`NodeRegistry`][mmisp.workflows.modules.NodeRegistry].
That way modules & triggers are registered
in the workflow application.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleParamType
documentation:
This enum provides supported form fields in the visual editor to
configure a parameter represented by
[`ModuleParam`][mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleParam] for a
module.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.Overhead

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.Overhead.from_int
documentation:
Returns a member of this enum given the int
representation of the overhead.

Arguments:
    input: numeric representation of the overhead.

Represents overhead of a module. That means e.g. how often it will be
executed on a typical MISP installation.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleParam

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleParam.id
documentation:
Identifier for the parameter. Must be unique in the context
of a single module.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleParam.label
documentation: Human-readable label in the visual editor for this parameter.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleParam.kind
documentation:
Which type of input is expected. Denoted by
[`ModuleParamType`][mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleParamType].

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleParam.options
documentation:
Additional options passed to the visual editor. The other
options are useful for e.g. validation of actual workflow
inputs. All the other parameters (e.g. placeholder) are
passed into this dictionary.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleParam.jinja_supported
documentation:
If `True`, the input from the visual editor for this parameter
is a jinja2 template. A template gets the
[`WorkflowInput`][mmisp.workflows.input.WorkflowInput] data
as input.

Each module can be configured in the visual editor, e.g. an
if/else module needs an operator, a value to check against and
an attribute-path to a the value to check against. All of these
values are configurable.

This class represents a single parameter passed to a module.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleConfiguration

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleConfiguration.validate
documentation:
Check if the parameters specified here are correct. For e.g. a
`select`-param with id "foobar", it will be checked if
`data["foobar"]` is among the options provided by the select.

Arguments:
    structure: The module param definitions to validate the
        configuration against.

class_variables.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleConfiguration.data
documentation:
The dictionary containing values for the parameters a module
needs.

Parameters for a module. If a module defines a textarea with ID
`foobar` as param, this class expects a dictionary

```python
{
    "foobar": "mytext"
}
```

These params are defined in the visual editor and thus saved
together with the module.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleAction
documentation:
Marker class representing an action module. Not relevant for the behavior,
but for the HTTP responses to determine which kind of module this is.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleLogic
documentation:
Marker class representing a logic module. Not relevant for the behavior,
but for the HTTP responses to determine which kind of module this is.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleIf

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleIf._exec
documentation:
The implementation of the if/else execution. The first return value indicates whether the execution was
successful, the second the decision whether to go to the yes or to the no next node.
The class on which all if/else module implementations will be based.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleConcurrentTask
documentation:
Accepts multiple connecting nodes and executes all of them
concurrently.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleGenericFilterData
documentation:
Configure a filter on the workflow payload. Every
subsequent module will only see the filtered version
unless the effect gets reversed with
[ModuleGenericFilterReset][mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleGenericFilterReset].

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleGenericFilterReset
documentation: Resets all filters declared for the workflow payload.

classes.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleOrganisationIf
documentation:
Module allowing to check if the organistaion property
of the payload matches a condition.

methods.name: mmisp.workflows.modules.ModuleTagOperation.initialize_for_visual_editor
documentation: supported: bool = False
